lead: After almost two decades moving families across this county, our team has carried furniture into riverside cottages, town terraces and clifftop homes alike. People often ask us where they ought to settle once the boxes are unpacked, so we have pulled together an honest tour of some of the most loved places to live in Suffolk. There is no single best, only the right fit for your life, and we hope this helps you picture where you might belong.

sections.h: Bury St Edmunds: A Historic Market Town With Everyday Convenience
sections.body:
If you want a town that feels alive yet keeps its sense of history, Bury St Edmunds is hard to beat. We move a great many people here, and the appeal is obvious the moment you arrive. The abbey gardens, the grand cathedral and the handsome Georgian streets give the centre a real dignity, while the regular markets keep it busy and sociable. It is the sort of place where you can run errands on foot and still feel you are somewhere special.

Practically speaking, the amenities are strong. There are good schools, a broad mix of shops, plenty of independent cafes and restaurants, and easy links onto the A14 for commuting east toward Ipswich or west toward Cambridge. Families settle here happily, and so do downsizers who want town life without the bustle of a city. The trade-off is that this popularity keeps demand high, so well-placed homes tend not to linger, and the most central spots come at a premium.

What stays with us, after so many moves into this corner of the county, is how welcoming it feels for newcomers. There is a genuine social rhythm to the place, from the seasonal events around the gardens to the everyday chatter of market mornings, and people seem to find their feet quickly. If you like the idea of a town with deep roots that still looks forward, Bury St Edmunds rewards you in ways that are hard to put a figure on.

sections.h: Woodbridge: Riverside Charm and a Sailing Way of Life
sections.body:
Woodbridge is one of our favourite places to deliver people to, partly because the setting is so gentle. Sitting on the River Deben, it has a relaxed, watery character that suits anyone who loves boats, walks along the quay and the slow rhythm of a tidal town. The famous Tide Mill and the moorings give the place its identity, and the period houses lining the streets above the river are full of charm.

The town itself is genuinely useful for daily living, with independent shops, a station on the East Suffolk line and a strong community feel. It tends to attract families, retirees and people who can work flexibly, drawn by the balance of pretty surroundings and proper amenities. We would be honest, though, that this combination makes it sought after, and the older riverside properties can need patient upkeep. For many, the lifestyle more than repays the effort.

One thing we always mention to people considering Woodbridge is how the river shapes daily life in small, pleasant ways. Mornings tend to begin with a wander down to the water, weekends fill up with sailing or simply watching the tide turn, and the surrounding countryside is laced with footpaths for those who would rather walk than mess about in boats. It is a place that quietly slows you down, and most people we move there tell us that is exactly what they were hoping for.

sections.h: Southwold: A Pretty Coastal Town With a Tight Community
sections.body:
Few places capture the seaside imagination quite like Southwold. The colourful beach huts, the lighthouse standing among the rooftops and the long pier make it instantly recognisable, and the sweep of the Suffolk Heritage Coast on its doorstep is glorious in every season. We have moved people here who simply fell for the light and the sea air and never looked back.

Beneath the postcard image is a real, working community with its greens, its shops and a strong local spirit that pulls together year-round. It suits those seeking a calmer, coastal pace, whether settling for good or making a long-held dream of a sea view come true. Be aware that Southwold is a popular destination, so the town can feel busier in the warmer months, and quieter once the visitors thin out in winter. If you love the coast in all its moods, that gentle ebb and flow is part of the pleasure.

sections.h: Aldeburgh: Where the Arts Meet the Sea
sections.body:
Aldeburgh has a character all of its own, shaped by the sea on one side and a long creative heritage on the other. The shingle beach, the fishing boats drawn up on the shore and the music festival tradition give the town a thoughtful, cultured atmosphere that draws a particular kind of resident. We often find people moving here are looking for inspiration as much as a home.

The high street is compact but well stocked, with good food, galleries and the kind of independent shops that reward a slow wander. It tends to suit those who value culture, the outdoors and a strong sense of place, including many who choose it for a later chapter of life by the coast. As with much of this stretch of Suffolk, demand is keen and homes near the front are especially prized, so a little patience usually pays off when the right one appears.

Living in Aldeburgh means embracing a coast that is bracing and beautiful in equal measure, where the weather has its say and the sea is never far from your thoughts. We have found that the people who settle happily here are the ones who want that closeness to nature and culture rather than a busy high street. If you treasure long shingle walks, fresh fish straight off the boats and an evening of music, it is a genuinely special place to call home.

Step into Lavenham and you step back several centuries. This is one of the best preserved medieval villages in the country, and its leaning timber-framed houses, painted in soft Suffolk colours, give it an almost storybook quality. Moving someone into a home here always feels a little ceremonial, because so many of these properties carry real history in their beams and floors.

Life in Lavenham is quieter and more village-scaled than the towns, with a fine guildhall, characterful pubs and tea rooms, and the wider countryside close at hand. It appeals to people who love period homes and a strong sense of heritage, and who do not mind being a short drive from larger centres such as Bury St Edmunds for bigger shops and stations. Older houses can ask for careful, sympathetic maintenance, but for the right owner that responsibility is a privilege rather than a chore.

sections.h: Ipswich and Its Villages: County-Town Convenience and a Working Waterfront
sections.body:
As a Westerfield-based team just outside Ipswich, we know the county town and its surroundings better than anywhere. Ipswich offers the everyday practicality that the smaller towns and villages cannot always match, with a regenerated waterfront, a busy shopping centre, a mainline station with fast services toward London, and quick access to both the A12 and the A14. For commuters and those who want amenities on their doorstep, that convenience is a genuine draw.

What we love most, though, is the ring of villages around the town, ours included, where you can enjoy green lanes and a settled community while keeping the conveniences of Ipswich and the port at Felixstowe within easy reach. This blend suits families and working couples especially well. Parts of the town are livelier and more urban than the picture-perfect coast, so it pays to explore different neighbourhoods, but the variety on offer means most people find a corner that feels right for them.

faqs: Which part of Suffolk is best for commuting?

For commuting, the towns along the main road and rail corridors tend to work best. Ipswich has a mainline station with fast services toward London and sits where the A12 and A14 meet, while Bury St Edmunds gives easy access westward toward Cambridge. Woodbridge and other stops on the East Suffolk line suit those who prefer a smaller town with a rail link, though journeys may involve a connection.
Is coastal Suffolk a good place to live year-round?

Yes, but it helps to know what to expect. Towns such as Southwold and Aldeburgh have warm, settled communities that carry on happily through the winter, when the crowds have gone and the coast feels wonderfully peaceful. They are busier in the warmer months, so if you value quiet you may prefer a slightly inland spot, while still being a short drive from the Suffolk Heritage Coast whenever you fancy the sea.
Where should I look in Suffolk if I want a period or historic home?

Suffolk is rich in period property, so you are spoilt for choice. Lavenham is renowned for its medieval timber-framed houses, while Woodbridge and Bury St Edmunds both offer fine Georgian and older homes with plenty of character. Coastal towns add their own historic charm too. Do bear in mind that older homes often ask for careful, sympathetic upkeep, so it is worth planning for that alongside the joy of living somewhere with real history.
Do you cover moves to all of these Suffolk towns and villages?

We do. As a family-run team based in Westerfield near Ipswich and trading since 2005, we have moved people into every corner of the county, from riverside Woodbridge to clifftop Southwold and the lanes around Lavenham. Whether it is a village cottage or a town house, every move is fully covered and handled with care, and we are always happy to talk through the practicalities of relocating to your chosen part of Suffolk.
